project_overview.project_development_objective: To increase the efficiency and reliability of water services in selected areas of Tegucigalpa by contributing to: (i) the operationalization of a new service provider (UMAPS); and (ii) improvements in infrastructure and resource management tools.
project_overview.total_financing: US$126.0 million (US$50.0 million IDA credit, US$76.0 million counterpart funding)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Approximately 1 million people; directly benefiting at least 180,417 residents in Picacho pilot area; indirectly benefiting remaining 855,700 inhabitants of Tegucigalpa metropolitan area

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: no - Honduras is not classified as fragile or conflict-affected, though it faces significant governance and security challenges

key_components.description: Support for transfer of water and sanitation services from SANAA to new municipal provider (UMAPS) through governance reform, gender policy implementation, and transfer of administrative, financial, commercial, and operational functions. Includes severance payments for SANAA staff (US$89 million total, with US$18 million IDA financing).
key_components.financing: US$89.0 million total (US$18.0 million IDA, US$42.5 million Central Government, US$28.5 million AMDC)

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Improved water production capacity and efficiency of the distribution network
key_components.description: Three subcomponents: (2.1) Improving water availability through WTP rehabilitation/upgrading, district metered areas implementation, and NRW reduction in targeted areas; (2.2) Strengthening safety of Laureles and Concepción dams through comprehensive assessments, Panel of Experts oversight, and emergency preparedness; (2.3) Developing watershed management and climate resilience tools including sanitation planning and water quality monitoring.
key_components.financing: US$31.0 million total (US$27.0 million IDA, US$4.0 million AMDC)

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Project Management and Technical Assistance
key_components.description: Financing for project coordination, fiduciary management, environmental and social compliance, M&E, communications strategy, gender gap analysis, and capacity building. Includes hiring of Project Management Consultant and Independent Verification Agent.
key_components.financing: US$6.0 million total (US$5.0 million IDA, US$1.0 million AMDC)

key_components.description: Provides mechanism for immediate response to eligible emergencies through reallocation of project funds, as defined in CER Operational Manual.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Municipality of Tegucigalpa (AMDC) through Project Coordination Unit (PCU/AMDC), with UMAPS as the new service provider entity. Ministry of Finance (SEFIN) is the Recipient for credit purposes.
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Service Performance Agreement (SPA) between AMDC and UMAPS defining roles, responsibilities, and performance targets; coordination with Water and Sanitation Regulator (ERSAPS); Municipal Water and Sanitation Committee (COMAS) for citizen oversight; coordination with IDB, CABEI, and JICA on complementary investments
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Recipients (July 2016, revised November 2017 and August 2018); Project Procurement Strategy for Development (PPSD) with 18-month procurement plan; mix of international and national competitive bidding; prior and post-review thresholds applied; use of STEP system for procurement tracking
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: AMDC/PCU responsible for M&E with support from Project Management Consultant; Independent Verification Agent (IVA) verifies achievement of Disbursement-Linked Indicators (DLIs) twice per calendar year; semiannual Project Reports required within 45 days of reporting period; beneficiary assessment surveys on annual basis; external audit by private firm in accordance with International Standards on Auditing

identified_risks.risk_description: National-level political instability and uncertainty during electoral cycles and transitions have historically undermined macroeconomic stability and operational engagement. At municipal level, change in administration could alter priorities and undermine implementation.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Early and regular high-level dialogue with GoH, municipal authorities, and other multilaterals (IDB, CABEI); Service Performance Agreement (SPA) between AMDC and UMAPS with performance targets and modification mechanisms requiring Regulator approval and public consultations; ring-fencing of operation from political interference through governance reforms

identified_risks.risk_description: IMF concerns about fiscal sustainability; persistent deficit of state electricity company (ENEE) threatens Honduras' fiscal position; limited fiscal space may affect Project budget and viability; need for continued ambitious fiscal reforms and revenue mobilization.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Close tracking of macroeconomic issues with GoH; modification of Project disbursement projections if necessary; coordination with IDB on complementary policy-based lending to support institutional reforms

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: AMDC lacks previous experience implementing World Bank projects; innovative IPF/DLI instrument may prove challenging; risk of delays in procurement and DLI tracking; dependency on external consultants for verification and management.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: DLI workshop conducted during preparation to familiarize AMDC staff with mechanism; recruitment of experienced Project Coordinator; contracting of international Project Management Consultant (PMC) to support PCU; hiring of Independent Verification Agent (IVA) for DLI verification; close Bank supervision during first years; interim agreement between UMAPS and SANAA if IDB funding unavailable

identified_risks.risk_description: New PCU and AMDC lack experience with Bank operations, increasing risk of procurement delays and disbursement issues; AMDC procurement unit has limited experience with World Bank Procurement Regulations; coordination challenges between technical and procurement units; complexity of DLI-based disbursements.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Recruitment of experienced Project Coordinator and FM specialist with World Bank experience; contracting of international PMC for technical and operational support; hiring of IVA for DLI verification; strengthening of AMDC FM Unit; detailed procedures in Project Operational Manual (POM); Bank training and close supervision during implementation; annual post-procurement review supervision with minimum 20% sample review

identified_risks.risk_description: Civil society may react negatively to municipalization, mistaking it for privatization and refusing to pay for services; SANAA labor union may seek to delay transfer of operations; resistance to behavioral change regarding water conservation; potential for gender-based violence and labor disputes.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Comprehensive communications strategy and consultation plan emphasizing AMDC remains asset holder and UMAPS is public agent; stakeholder workshops, information campaigns, public hearings, and community dialogue; severance payments awarded prior to transfer in compliance with labor law; civil society representation in COMAS; gender policy implementation with training on gender equality; grievance redress mechanism; Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP) implementation

identified_risks.risk_description: Dam safety risks at Laureles and Concepción dams; potential impacts from climate change on water availability and dam structural integrity; land acquisition and involuntary resettlement risks; water quality and pollution issues; occupational health and safety risks during construction; potential impacts on downstream communities.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Comprehensive dam safety assessments including climate change impact analysis; Panel of Experts (POE) to review and advise on dam safety studies and works; dam safety action plans with instrumentation, O&M, and emergency preparedness; immediate minor remedial works and early warning systems; Environmental and Social Assessment (ESA) and Environmental and Social Management Plan (ESMP) prepared and disclosed; Environmental and Social Commitment Plan (ESCP) specifying implementation requirements; Labor Management Procedures; dam safety capacity building for UMAPS staff; environmental and social specialists in PCU; monitoring of compliance with Environmental and Social Standards

identified_risks.risk_description: Complexity of water infrastructure rehabilitation; potential for cost overruns in civil works; delays in WTP improvements affecting water availability gains; challenges in achieving NRW reduction targets; technical capacity of UMAPS to operate rehabilitated infrastructure.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project design based on comprehensive business plan and technical assessments; PMC to provide technical expertise and knowledge transfer; detailed engineering designs for all major works; supervision and quality assurance plans; capacity building and training for UMAPS staff; phased implementation approach allowing flexibility; performance-based contracts with knowledge transfer requirements

identified_risks.risk_description: Delays in transfer of functions from SANAA to UMAPS due to severance payment constraints; insufficient funding for remaining severance obligations; coordination challenges between SANAA and UMAPS during transition; operational disruptions during service transfer; inadequate management of AMDC/UMAPS reducing effectiveness of water recovery efforts.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Gradual transfer agreement allowing phased transfer as funding becomes available; coordination with IDB on policy-based loan for severance payments (US$30 million expected October 2019); Central Government commitment of US$30 million and AMDC commitment of US$28.5 million for severance; interim operational agreements between UMAPS and SANAA if needed; DLI-based disbursements incentivizing transfer completion; PMC support for operational management

triggered_safeguard_policies: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts (ESS1)
Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management (ESS4)
Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement (ESS5)
Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources (ESS6)
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Project-level grievance redress service (GRS) established; communities and individuals can submit complaints to project-level mechanisms or World Bank's Grievance Redress Service; World Bank Inspection Panel available for complaints regarding Bank non-compliance with policies; grievance resolution target within agreed timeframe is a project indicator
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Gender policy for UMAPS targeting 25% female participation in high-level decision-making and mid-level technical positions (up from 15% at SANAA); gender gap analysis to explore disparities related to WSS services in targeted areas; gender-sensitive recruitment campaigns; staff training on gender equality; career mentoring programs for women; equal pay for equal work provisions; gender-based violence prevention measures; women's organizations represented in COMAS and UMAPS Board of Directors; communications campaigns addressing gender-specific water collection burdens
vulnerable_groups: Low-income households (currently paying up to 250% of network tariff for tanker water)
Women and girls (bearing primary responsibility for water collection and domestic chores)
Peri-urban residents (segment 5 customers paying highest tariffs)
Downstream communities near Laureles and Concepción dams
SANAA staff to be dismissed (receiving severance payments)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project is not in a conflict-affected area but operates in context of high crime, gang violence, and institutional weakness. Design incorporates conflict-sensitive approaches: (1) Focus on institutional strengthening and governance reform to build state capacity and legitimacy; (2) Emphasis on transparency, accountability, and citizen participation through COMAS and public consultations to build trust in public institutions; (3) Communications strategy to counter misinformation about municipalization and build public support; (4) Service Performance Agreement and regulator oversight to reduce political interference and ensure professional management; (5) Gender policy and women's participation to promote inclusive governance; (6) Grievance redress mechanisms to address community concerns peacefully
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Honduras faces significant fragility factors: (1) Extreme poverty (64% of households, 40.7% in extreme poverty - second only to Haiti in LAC region); (2) High inequality (highest in LAC); (3) Weak institutional capacity and governance challenges; (4) High crime and gang violence affecting service delivery; (5) Political instability during electoral cycles; (6) Fiscal sustainability concerns; (7) Rapid unplanned urbanization (3.2% urban growth rate) outpacing service provision; (8) Climate vulnerability (most affected by climate change 1996-2015, losing 2.6% GDP annually to climatic events); (9) Water scarcity and environmental degradation; (10) Decentralization process incomplete, with institutional reforms stalled. Project addresses fragility by: strengthening municipal capacity, improving service delivery to reduce poverty, building institutional legitimacy through transparency and participation, enhancing climate resilience, and completing sector reforms
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security risks include: (1) Gang violence and organized crime affecting project areas and staff safety; (2) Potential labor unrest from SANAA union opposing staff dismissals; (3) Community resistance to service transfer if perceived as privatization; (4) Downstream safety risks from dam failures affecting ~2,000 people; (5) Risks to field staff during network rehabilitation in high-crime areas. Mitigation: (1) Dam safety measures including emergency preparedness plans and early warning systems; (2) Severance payments to address labor concerns; (3) Comprehensive communications and stakeholder engagement to build support; (4) Security protocols for project staff; (5) Coordination with local authorities; (6) Grievance mechanisms to address community concerns
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: No - Project does not involve involuntary resettlement or displacement. However, ESS5 (Land Acquisition and Involuntary Resettlement) is triggered as relevant due to: (1) Potential minor land requirements for WTP improvements and network rehabilitation; (2) Informal settlements along water aqueducts (Jutiapa aqueduct) with unregistered connections; (3) Potential restrictions on land use in watershed protection areas. Mitigation: Environmental and Social Assessment (ESA) and Environmental and Social Management Plan (ESMP) prepared; detailed site-specific planning for activities designed later will include corresponding ESA/ESMP development with stakeholder consultation and disclosure prior to implementation; watershed management plans will include land use regulation with community engagement
